d: Select a frame or multiple elements and press Shift+A to add auto layout. If elements are selected without a parent frame, Figma wraps them in a new auto layout frame. Can also right-click > Add auto layout.
Right-click an auto layout frame > Remove auto layout. This converts it back to a regular frame with absolute positioning. Children retain their current positions but are no longer flow-controlled.

Auto layout frames arrange children in a single direction (horizontal or vertical), or in a grid (2025+). Children are positioned based on direction, alignment, spacing (gap), and padding. Auto layout replaces manual positioning with flow-based layout similar to CSS Flexbox.

Auto layout replaces constraints on direct children. Children of an auto layout frame cannot use left/right/top/bottom constraints — they are positioned by the auto layout flow instead. Absolute-positioned children within auto layout are the exception and do use constraints.

Horizontal auto layout arranges children left to right in a row. Equivalent to CSS flexbox row direction. Toggle in the auto layout panel or press the horizontal arrow icon. Children stack side by side with the specified gap between them.

Vertical auto layout arranges children top to bottom in a column. Equivalent to CSS flexbox column direction. Toggle in the auto layout panel or press the vertical arrow icon. Children stack on top of each other with the specified gap between them.

Wrap mode causes children to flow to the next row or column when the container width is exceeded. Enable with Shift+Enter or toggle in auto layout panel. Creates multi-row/column layouts. Wrap has its own gap setting for the cross-axis (row gap vs column gap). Essential for responsive card grids.

Grid auto layout enables two-dimensional layout with explicit rows and columns. Change layout mode to Grid in auto layout settings. Define column and row tracks with fixed px, fractional fr, or auto sizing. Elements can span multiple columns and rows. Generates valid CSS Grid in dev mode. Use for image galleries, bento boxes, dashboards, product grids.

Packed is the default spacing mode. A fixed gap value in pixels is applied uniformly between all children. Set the gap value in the auto layout panel. Equivalent to CSS gap property. Gap applies only between children, not before the first or after the last child.

Space Between distributes children evenly across available space within the frame. Set gap to 'Auto' in the spacing dropdown. The first and last children align to the frame edges (after padding), and remaining space is divided equally between items. Equivalent to CSS justify-content: space-between.

Negative gap values cause children to overlap each other — useful for stacked avatars or card fan effects. Individual spacing override: hold Alt and drag between two specific items to set a custom gap for that pair only, overriding the frame-level gap. The override appears as a purple value in the panel.

Hug Contents causes the frame to shrink to the smallest size that fits its children plus padding. It is the default sizing mode for new auto layout frames. The frame dynamically resizes as children change. RULE: a frame cannot hug on an axis if any child is set to Fill Container on that same axis.

Fill Container causes a child to stretch and fill all available space within its parent auto layout frame on that axis. Critical for responsive design. Multiple fill children share available space equally. IMPORTANT: if any child is set to Fill, the parent cannot use Hug Contents on that axis — the parent must be Fixed or Fill itself.

Fixed sizing sets an explicit width or height in pixels. The element will not resize with its parent or children. Use sparingly — prefer Hug or Fill for flexible layouts. Fixed is appropriate for icons, avatars, known-dimension images, or intentionally rigid elements like sidebars.

A parent can Hug on one axis and have children Fill on the other axis. Example: a vertical auto layout frame that hugs its height (shrinks to content) while children fill the width. This is the foundation of flexible, responsive layouts. Common pattern: vertical frame hugs height, children fill width for full-width cards or sections.

Set min-width, max-width, min-height, max-height on any auto layout child. Access by clicking the width or height field dropdown and selecting 'Add min width' etc. A visual indicator (two lines on the dimension icon) appears when min/max is set. Use cases: responsive text containers that do not get too narrow or too wide, flexible cards with minimum sizes, constrained images. Min/max values are respected within the auto layout sizing mode.

Padding adds internal space between the frame edge and its children. Uniform padding applies one value to all four sides. Click the padding icon to toggle independent padding with separate Top, Right, Bottom, Left fields. Padding does NOT affect the gap between children. Shorthand in UI shows T/R/B/L values. Padding is equivalent to CSS padding property.

Auto layout uses a 3x3 alignment grid controlling where children sit within the frame: top-left, top-center, top-right, middle-left, center, middle-right, bottom-left, bottom-center, bottom-right. This controls both the main axis (justify) and cross axis (align) simultaneously. Maps to combinations of CSS justify-content and align-items.

Individual children can override the parent frame's alignment using Align Self. Access via right-click > Align or in the design panel when a child is selected. This allows one child to be top-aligned while others are centered, for example. Equivalent to CSS align-self property.

Text baseline alignment aligns children based on the text baseline of text layers within them. Useful when mixing text of different font sizes or text alongside icons/buttons so that the text lines up visually. Enable in the alignment options in the auto layout panel.

Any direct child of an auto layout frame can be set to Absolute position in the design panel. Absolute children are removed from the auto layout flow but remain visually within the frame bounds. They do not affect spacing or sizing of other children. Use for overlapping badges, floating action buttons, decorative elements, notification dots. Absolute children respond to constraints (left, right, top, bottom, center) relative to the parent frame.

Navbar: horizontal auto layout frame. Children: logo (fixed width) + nav-links (horizontal auto layout, fill container to push items apart) + CTA button (fixed or hug). Set parent to space-between or use fill on the middle section. Padding 16-24px horizontal. Align center vertically. Frame set to fill width of page.

Card: vertical auto layout frame. Children: image (fixed height, fill width) + content area (vertical auto layout, fill container, padding 16-24px, gap 8-12px) + optional footer (horizontal auto layout, fill width). Card frame uses hug height with fixed or fill width. Border radius 8-16px. Common gap between sections: 0 or 16px.

Form: vertical auto layout frame. Each field is a vertical auto layout with label (hug) + input (fill width, fixed height ~40-48px). Fields are stacked with gap 16-24px. Submit button at bottom with fill or hug width. Use min-width 280px and max-width 480px on the form frame for responsive sizing.

Sidebar layout: horizontal auto layout frame (fill width, fill height). Children: sidebar (fixed width 240-320px, fill height, vertical auto layout) + main content (fill container on both axes, vertical auto layout). Main content area scrolls or contains nested auto layout sections. Add a 1px divider between sidebar and content or use gap 0 with border on sidebar.

Full page layout: vertical auto layout frame (fill width, fill height). Children: header (fill width, hug or fixed height) + content (fill container, expands to take remaining space) + footer (fill width, hug or fixed height). This creates a sticky footer effect where footer always stays at the bottom. Content fills the vertical space between header and footer.

Grid auto layout tracks define column and row sizes. Fixed px for exact dimensions, fractional fr units for proportional sizing (like CSS fr), auto for content-based sizing. Example: 3-column equal grid uses 1fr 1fr 1fr. Mixed: 200px 1fr 1fr creates a fixed sidebar with two flexible columns. Tracks are configured in the auto layout panel under Grid settings.

In grid auto layout, elements can span multiple columns and/or rows. Select an element in the grid and set its column span or row span in the design panel. Use for hero sections spanning full width, featured cards taking 2x2 cells, or sidebar elements spanning all rows. Equivalent to CSS grid-column: span N / grid-row: span N.

Freeform mode allows free positioning of children inside an auto layout frame at arbitrary coordinates. Children can be placed anywhere without following flow rules. The frame itself retains auto layout benefits like padding and resizing behavior. Useful for complex overlapping designs, creative layouts, or transitional states where full flow control is not needed.

Mobile-to-desktop responsive pattern: use horizontal auto layout with wrap enabled. Items have fixed or min-width. On wide screens, items sit in a row. As the frame narrows, items wrap to the next line. Combine with space-between or packed spacing. Set gap for both horizontal and vertical (row gap). This eliminates the need for separate mobile and desktop frames in many cases.

For responsive text blocks: set text layer to fill width within auto layout. Apply min-width (e.g., 200px) to prevent text from becoming too narrow and max-width (e.g., 600px) to maintain readable line lengths. The text container flexes between these bounds as the parent resizes. Essential for readable body text in responsive designs.

Recommended spacing values: Page padding — 16px mobile, 24px tablet, 32-64px desktop. Card internal padding — 16-24px. Section vertical gap — 24-48px. Element gap within sections — 8-16px. Button padding — 12-16px vertical, 24-32px horizontal. Input field padding — 12-16px. List item gap — 8-12px. Use multiples of 4 or 8 for consistency (4px grid).

t: Rules: Groups, Spacers, and Fixed Sizes
d: RULE: Never use groups inside auto layout — always convert to frames. Groups do not have defined dimensions and break auto layout sizing. RULE: Avoid spacer frames — use padding and gap instead. RULE: Avoid fixed sizes unless necessary (icons, avatars, known dimensions). Prefer hug and fill for flexible, maintainable layouts. RULE: All direct children of auto layout must be layers; you cannot directly edit text on the frame itself.

Double-click an auto layout frame edge to hug contents on that axis. Hold Ctrl/Cmd while resizing an auto layout frame to temporarily ignore auto layout and resize freely. Swap direction between horizontal and vertical using the arrow icons in the auto layout panel. Press Shift+A on nested elements to add auto layout to their immediate parent. Use the auto layout panel's three-dot menu for advanced options.

Use negative gap values to overlap children — common for stacked avatar groups (gap -8 to -12px) or card fan effects. For more complex overlaps, use absolute positioning within auto layout. Combine negative gap with a specific stacking order (first child on top or bottom) controlled by layer order. Negative gap works in both horizontal and vertical directions.

ALWAYS build layouts so that resizing the top-level frame cascades correctly to every child — no manual repositioning, no broken text, no orphaned elements. This is the default build standard. The mental model: design top-down with relative sizing. Every frame should answer the question 'what happens when my parent changes size?' before it is finalized. Corollary: if you have to manually move anything after resizing a frame, the layout is wrong. Strategy: top-level frame (Fixed or Fill) → section frames (Fill width, Hug height) → inner frames (Fill or Hug per axis based on content) → leaf elements (Fill width for text and full-width elements, Fixed only for icons/avatars/images with known dimensions).

t: Text Sizing Rule: Fill Container, Never Fixed
d: RULE: Text layers inside auto layout frames must ALWAYS have their width set to Fill Container, not Fixed. Fixed-width text breaks layouts when the parent resizes — text gets clipped or creates overflow. The only exception is text in a badge, tag, or pill where the text should hug its own content (set to Hug). For body copy, labels, headings, captions, input text, and any text that should respond to its container: Fill Container on width. Height always Hug (auto) unless the element has a deliberate fixed-height constraint like a single-line input field.

meta.rule: Text width = Fill Container. Text height = Hug. Fixed width on text = almost always wrong.

t: Resize Cascade Checklist — Verify Before Handoff
d: Before handing off or considering a layout done, perform this resize test: (1) Select the top-level frame and drag its width narrower and wider — nothing should overflow or overlap. (2) Select the top-level frame and drag its height — content sections should expand/contract, not stay frozen. (3) Double-click into each text layer and add a line of text — the parent frame should grow, not clip. (4) Check every text layer: width should read Fill, not a fixed px value. (5) Check every inner section frame: at least one axis should be Fill or Hug, not both Fixed. If any of these tests fail, trace back to the first layer that has a fixed dimension it should not have and switch it to Fill or Hug.
